text.title: Multiple Input Modalities and Visual Accessibility in XR
text.summary: Input modality requirements for spatial interfaces (gaze, voice, head tracking, controller, hand gestures) and visual accessibility patterns for blind and low-vision users.

rule.statement: Provide all input modalities simultaneously (gaze + voice + controller + hand gesture) — never require users to switch modality mid-task, and never make any single modality the only path to complete an action.
rule.rationale: W3C XAUR requires multiple input alternatives. Gaze requires eye-tracking hardware (Vision Pro yes; Quest 2 no); voice degrades in noisy environments; controller excludes users with limited arm mobility. Only providing all options simultaneously guarantees coverage.

example.code: Interaction | Touch/Controller | Voice | Gaze+Dwell Select object | Tap / trigger | "Select [name]" | Look for 1.5s Move object | Drag / joystick | "Move to [loc]" | Dwell + direction Open menu | Button press | "Show menu" | Look at menu icon Cancel | B button / back | "Cancel" | Look away

list.item: Dwell time: 1-2 seconds; show visual countdown ring on gaze target.
Confirmation before any irreversible action — cancel by looking away.
Gaze requires eye-tracking hardware; check platform capability at runtime before enabling.

rule.statement: Every spatial interaction must have a named voice-command alternative; publish a visible command list in-world.
rule.rationale: Voice commands must be discoverable; if users cannot find the command vocabulary, voice is not actually an accessible path. On-device ASR keeps response latency under 500ms — cloud LLM calls violate this threshold.

list.item: Voice command response must feel <500ms — use on-device ASR, not cloud LLM round-trip.
Provide error recovery: "I didn't understand. Say 'Help' to see available commands."
Test with diverse accents; generated grammars miss accent variants.
Nod to confirm, shake to cancel, tilt to scroll — always provide a voice or controller alternative.
Limit head-tracking interactions: neck strain risk with sustained use.
Allow sensitivity adjustment in accessibility settings.

rule.statement: Attach directional audio beacons to spatial landmarks (entrance, exit, key interactive objects) with distinct timbres so blind users can navigate by sound.
rule.rationale: Without spatial audio wayfinding, blind users have no equivalent to visual scanning. Distinct timbres per object type allow users to build a mental map of the environment.

list.item: Directional audio cues: volume indicates distance, stereo panning indicates direction.
AI-powered object descriptions: announce objects in view, describe spatial relationships, read text encountered, warn of obstacles.
Haptic feedback: vibration for object proximity, different patterns for object types.
Screen reader integration (where platform supports it): announce spatial UI elements, navigate menus with voice.
High contrast mode in 3D space — adjustable contrast for AR/VR overlays.
Large text in spatial UI — adjustable size and distance from user.
Color-blind safe palettes for all spatial indicators.
Zoom functionality within the XR environment.
